name: Jasmine Crowe-Houston
title: Feeding More, Wasting Less: The Jasmine Crowe-Houston Playbook
dek: She turned a dinner party for the homeless into a tech company that treats food waste like the logistics problem it actually is. Spoiler: it works.
body_html:
Most people see a dumpster behind a restaurant and see trash. Jasmine Crowe-Houston saw dinner for thousands. That reframe, that perfectly good food is not waste but misrouted inventory, is the spark behind Goodr, a company that took one of the world's most maddening contradictions, full dumpsters and empty stomachs, and attacked it with software, trucks, and a whole lot of nerve.

The Come-Up: From Pop-Up Dinners to a Tech Startup

Before there was an app, there was a folding table. Jasmine started by hosting pop-up dinners for people experiencing homelessness, cooking and serving restaurant-quality meals with dignity, sometimes documenting it to wake people up to the scale of the problem. Up close, she clocked the real issue: it wasn't a shortage of food, it was a shortage of logistics. Tons of perfectly edible surplus was getting tossed by businesses simply because nobody had built the pipes to move it somewhere useful.

So she built the pipes. Jasmine launched Goodr to connect businesses with surplus food to the communities that needed it, reframing charity as a supply-chain solution. As a Black woman founder in tech, she has been candid about how steep the fundraising climb was, and how she kept building anyway.

The Creativity: Treating Hunger Like a Data Problem

Here's the clever core. Goodr isn't a soup kitchen with a logo. It's a technology platform. Businesses get a way to redirect surplus food, complete with the tracking and reporting they care about, think tax and sustainability data, the diversion metrics, the audit trail. The food gets routed to nonprofits and communities efficiently rather than left to spoil. Everybody's incentives finally point the same direction.

She didn't ask companies to be charitable. She made doing the right thing the easy, trackable, good-for-business thing.

That's the marketing genius hiding inside the mission. By giving corporations clean dashboards and ESG-friendly receipts for their surplus, Goodr turned a feel-good act into an operational no-brainer. The brand itself is warm and direct, built around a simple, sticky promise of feeding more and wasting less, and Jasmine became a magnetic storyteller for it, including a widely shared TED-style talk that put the absurdity of food waste in front of huge audiences. She made an unsexy logistics problem genuinely compelling.

The Growth: Scaling Impact Into a Real Business

Goodr scaled by landing serious clients, reportedly working with major venues, corporations, and institutions to manage their surplus food and reduce waste at meaningful scale. The company has been credited with diverting large volumes of food from landfills and redirecting millions of meals to people who needed them, while building a genuine revenue-generating business rather than relying on donations alone. Jasmine has raised funding, collected recognition across food-tech and social-impact circles, and become a go-to voice on building mission-driven companies that actually make money.

The throughline is refreshingly unsentimental: she proved that fighting hunger and running a smart, growing business are not opposites. They can be the exact same spreadsheet. For a generation that wants its work to mean something and pay, Jasmine Crowe-Houston is a walking proof of concept.

She looked at the dumpster, did the math, and fed a movement.
